description: Curated list of Cowork plugins. A plugin is a host-side helper process the Electron main spawns OUTSIDE the bubblewrap sandbox. Plugins manage host resources (e.g. a real browser) and expose local endpoints so sandboxed `claude` turns (and any MCP server) can reach them without needing the sandbox to be widened. Settings declared here are rendered dynamically by the Plugins tab in Settings; runtime values are persisted under ~/.cowork/plugins.json (mode 0600).

plugins.name: Chrome Controller
plugins.description: Launches a managed Chrome instance on the host with the Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) enabled. Chrome cannot be launched from inside a chat turn because the sandbox hides X11, Wayland, and dbus; this plugin runs on the host instead and exposes a CDP endpoint on loopback (http://127.0.0.1:<port>). Point an MCP server like chrome-devtools-mcp at that URL via --browserUrl and it can drive real pages with real logins.

settings.label: Chrome binary
settings.description: Absolute path to the Chrome executable, or the command name if it is on $PATH. Leave blank to auto-detect (google-chrome, google-chrome-stable, chromium, chromium-browser, chrome).

settings.label: CDP port
settings.description: Port Chrome listens on for --remote-debugging-port. Bound to 127.0.0.1 only. If a Chrome is already answering CDP on this port, the plugin adopts it instead of starting a duplicate.

settings.label: Run headless
settings.description: Launch Chrome without a visible window (--headless=new). Handy on servers, in VMs, or when the browser is purely for automation.

settings.label: User data directory
settings.description: Path to the Chrome profile this plugin uses. Leave blank for a dedicated Cowork profile at ~/.cache/cowork/chrome-<port>. A dedicated profile is recommended — without one, Chrome's singleton lock silently hands our launch off to your daily Chrome and --remote-debugging-port gets ignored. Only override if you deliberately want to share a profile, in which case close any other Chrome using that profile first.

plugins.notes: Runs on the host, not inside the sandbox. The CDP endpoint is bound to loopback only, so only local processes can drive the browser. By default the plugin uses a fresh profile under ~/.cache/cowork — no shared logins, no conflict with your daily Chrome. If you point it at a profile your daily Chrome uses, assume any session that browser is logged into (email, banking, work SSO) is reachable by whatever attaches to CDP.
